summary:
The Russian Civil War (1917–1922) was a multi-sided armed conflict that followed the [[event:2646|October Revolution]], pitting the Bolshevik Red Army against a loose coalition of anti-Bolshevik White forces, foreign interventionist armies, and various nationalist and anarchist factions. The war determined whether the Bolshevik seizure of power would survive or be reversed, and its outcome shaped the entire trajectory of the twentieth century by establishing the [[event:111|Soviet Union]] as a permanent political reality.

The White armies were diverse in ideology and leadership — they included tsarist officers, liberal democrats, Socialist Revolutionaries, and Cossack forces — but this very diversity prevented effective coordination. Armies under Generals Denikin, Kolchak, Yudenich, and Wrangel operated on separate fronts without a unified command or consistent political program. Foreign powers — Britain, France, the United States, Japan, and others — intervened with troops and supplies, motivated partly by a desire to bring Russia back into [[event:110|World War I]] and partly by ideological hostility to Bolshevism. Their involvement proved insufficient to tip the balance but reinforced the Bolsheviks' nationalist narrative of defending Russia against foreign enemies.

Leon Trotsky's organization of the Red Army was a formidable achievement, transforming a collection of revolutionary militias into a disciplined fighting force of five million soldiers. The Bolsheviks benefited from controlling the densely populated and industrialized core of Russia, including Moscow and Petrograd, while the Whites operated from the periphery. Bolshevik political commissars maintained ideological discipline; mass conscription and brutal coercion filled the ranks. By 1922, the White armies had been defeated, foreign forces withdrew, and Bolshevik power was secured — at a cost of perhaps 12 million lives from combat, famine, and disease.
